text.title: External Service Mocking
text.summary: respx for lightweight httpx route mocking in unit/integration tests, WireMock via Testcontainers for realistic outbound HTTP simulation, and the antipatterns table.

rule.statement: Use @respx.mock to intercept outbound httpx requests in tests; define route → response mappings before calling the code under test; assert call counts and request bodies after.
rule.rationale: respx operates at the transport layer, intercepting real httpx calls without any code changes. This is the minimal-overhead way to mock HTTP dependencies in Python async code.

rule.statement: Use a WireMock container (session-scoped) when the code under test calls multiple endpoints, uses HTTP features (redirects, cookies, chunked transfer), or when you need realistic HTTP behavior that respx cannot simulate.
rule.rationale: WireMock runs a real HTTP server; the application connects to it exactly as it would to the real service. This catches connection pooling bugs, header parsing issues, and retry logic that mock transports miss.

antipattern.description: Testing against a production database or live external API in CI.
antipattern.reason: Network failures and data mutations cause flaky tests and real side effects. Use Testcontainers for databases and respx/WireMock for HTTP dependencies.

antipattern.description: Mocking the integration layer to test integration behavior.
antipattern.reason: If you mock the database in an "integration test", you are writing a unit test. Integration tests must use real (or containerized) services to validate the actual connection.

antipattern.description: Sharing mutable test data across integration tests without rollback or cleanup.
antipattern.reason: Tests become order-dependent. One test's inserted row can cause another test's unique constraint violation, creating intermittent failures that are hard to reproduce.
